claim: Prompt engineering fundamentally involves clear communication with the model about task requirements; the 'engineering' aspect comes from the iterative experimentation and trial-and-error process enabled by the ability to restart from a clean slate with each iteration.
actionable: When writing prompts, treat each iteration as a discrete experiment; reset to a blank state between attempts and vary only specific elements to isolate what improves performance.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Being a good prompt engineer requires the ability to iterate, think about edge cases and failure modes ahead of time, and test prompts against unusual or unexpected inputs rather than just the happy path.
actionable: Before considering a prompt complete, systematically identify 5-10 edge cases (empty inputs, missing data, malformed inputs) and test the prompt against each; refine the prompt to handle these cases explicitly.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Reading model outputs closely is essential to evaluating prompts; many people use techniques like 'think step-by-step' but never verify that the model is actually thinking step-by-step in the way they intended.
actionable: After updating a prompt, manually read 5-10 complete model outputs before declaring success; verify that the model is producing reasoning in the format and detail level you actually intended.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: When a model makes a mistake on a task, asking it to explain why it failed and then requesting an edited version of the instructions often results in the model correctly identifying the ambiguity and providing a fix that works.
actionable: When a prompt fails, ask the model: 'You got this wrong. Why? Can you write an edited version of these instructions so you get it right?' Use the model's suggested edits as your updated prompt.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: For research prompts, using few examples or intentionally non-representative examples is preferable because concrete examples cause models to latch onto and replicate the specific format, constraining the diversity of exploration needed for research.
actionable: For research tasks, use 1-2 deliberately dissimilar examples from the actual data you'll use (e.g., children's story examples for document extraction tasks); avoid examples that match your real data's format or domain.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: For enterprise/production prompts, adding many examples increases reliability and consistency by anchoring the model to desired outputs; this differs from research use where consistent output is less valuable than diverse exploration.
actionable: For production systems, build 10-50+ diverse examples into the prompt; for research exploration, limit to 1-2 deliberately non-representative examples.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: The step-by-step reasoning technique (chain-of-thought) was specifically trained into Claude for math tasks; you no longer need to explicitly request step-by-step reasoning for math, though you can still structure reasoning for other tasks.
actionable: For math problems, assume the model will think step-by-step by default; spend your prompting effort on other aspects. For non-math tasks, explicitly request structured reasoning when beneficial.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: When describing a task to a model, pretend you're explaining it to a competent temp agency worker who knows nothing about your specific context; describe the task directly without assuming prior knowledge or using implicit assumptions.
actionable: Before finalizing a prompt, ask yourself: 'Could a competent stranger do this task with just my written instructions?' Expand any implicit assumptions, jargon, or context that you naturally understand but the model doesn't.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Honesty about task context and goals is preferred over persona-based prompting; instead of saying 'You are a teacher', directly state 'I want you to construct questions for a language model evaluation' because the model understands the actual task better than analogies.
actionable: Write prompts that directly name the real task, domain, and goal instead of assuming a persona will help; if a persona helps, use it as a framing metaphor ('grade this like a high school teacher would') rather than a role assertion.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Metaphors and analogies can help models understand desired analysis frames (e.g., 'grade this chart like a high school teacher') but should be used to convey the scale/thinking style you want, not as a substitute for describing the actual task.
actionable: Use metaphors sparingly to establish the evaluation scale or thinking style ('imagine you're grading this as a high school chart'), but always also explicitly describe what you want the output to be.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: When prompts fail, provide the model with explicit fallback instructions for ambiguous cases; for example, 'If you're unsure, output <unsure></unsure>' helps capture edge cases and improves data quality by surfacing difficult inputs.
actionable: Add to prompts: 'If this is unclear or doesn't match the expected format, respond with <unsure></unsure> and explain why.' Review all unsure outputs to identify edge cases in your prompt.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Pretrained models show much higher conditional probability of typos following typos in pretraining data, while RLHF models actively suppress typos; this explains why typo-related prompting techniques work for pretrained but not RLHF models.
actionable: For RLHF models like Claude, don't use typos as a prompting technique; if you need to test model robustness, use the pretrained version of the model instead.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: RLHF-trained models have learned to infer user intent from subtle cues in the query rather than blindly following the most literal interpretation, unlike pretrained models which continue text completion probabilistically.
actionable: When writing prompts for RLHF models, you can use subtle context and intent-signaling language; the model will pick up on what you actually want rather than the most literal parsing of instructions.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: As models improve, many prompt engineering techniques get trained directly into the model behavior, making those tricks unnecessary; examples and chain-of-thought are lasting techniques because they represent core communication principles, not model quirks.
actionable: Focus prompt engineering effort on fundamental communication principles (clarity, structure, examples) rather than clever tricks, which have short shelf lives as models improve.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: You can give a model a research paper on a prompting technique and ask it to generate examples or write a meta-prompt implementing that technique, rather than manually replicating the technique from description.
actionable: When you want to test a new prompting technique, find the research paper, give it to the model with instructions: 'Here's a paper on [technique]. Write me a prompt template that applies this technique.' The model can directly implement techniques from literature.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: The most effective way to learn prompt engineering is to push the boundaries of what you think the model can do; trivial tasks that models handle easily teach little, while attempting very difficult tasks reveals how models actually work.
actionable: Deliberately seek out tasks you think might be at the edge of the model's capabilities; spend time trying to get the model to do something you're skeptical it can do—you'll learn more than from tasks within easy reach.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Visual prompting requires different intuitions than text prompting; few-shot examples are less effective for images, and detailed structural scaffolding (grids, ASCII representation) is often needed to improve visual understanding.
actionable: When prompting for image understanding, don't assume text prompting intuitions transfer; use structural descriptions (grid-based layouts, ASCII representation) and accept that image prompting may require more explicit scaffolding.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: When you've tried many prompt variations on a task without success, don't indefinitely grind on prompting; after getting from 'no signal' to 'some signal' with reasonable effort, waiting for a better model version is often a better use of time.
actionable: Set a reasonable effort ceiling (e.g., 4-8 hours) for pushing a single task; if you're still far from success, pause and reassess whether the task is truly within the current model's capability or if a model upgrade is needed.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: For well-constructed prompts tested across diverse edge cases (few hundred examples), the model's consistency is more informative than looking at thousands of loosely-crafted examples; high-signal prompts reduce noise better than quantity.
actionable: Build 100-300 carefully-selected edge cases and test your prompt against all of them; one well-crafted evaluation beats looser evaluation on larger datasets because it gives higher signal per example.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: Philosophy training (specifically the discipline of writing clearly for educated laypersons) transfers well to prompt engineering; the core skill is externalizing complex ideas in legible form to an intelligent but uninformed reader.
actionable: When writing prompts, adopt the philosophy discipline approach: assume the reader is intelligent but knows nothing about your domain; make every complex idea legible without talking down; be precise without assuming prior knowledge.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive

claim: In the future, as models become more capable, the balance will shift from users teaching models (explaining the task) to models interviewing users (eliciting what the user actually wants), requiring a shift from prompt writing to introspection about goals.
actionable: Start practicing having the model interview you about your goals before attempting to write prompts; frame prompting as a collaborative elicitation process rather than one-directional instruction.
source_title: AI prompt engineering: A deep dive
